<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: agency-dignity-and-corrigibility
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/agency-dignity-and-corrigibility.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/agency-dignity-and-corrigibility.qmd@08a756e54
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility

A constitution is not real until a person can use it.

The previous chapter translated constitutional commitments into predicates,
review routes, and migration records. This chapter asks whether those
commitments reach the human being standing near the system. Can the affected
person refuse? Can they understand the action before it matters? Can they
appeal, correct, reverse, audit, or exit? Can they do those things without
needing to out-argue the system on its own technical ground?

That is why agency, dignity, and corrigibility are treated here as interfaces,
not moods. A system is not corrigible because it sounds cooperative. It is
corrigible when correction paths remain available after deployment pressure,
memory updates, delegation, automation, and capability replacement make
correction inconvenient.

Dignity becomes operational when the system keeps usable handles in place at
the moments when power is about to act. A right that disappears under pressure
is not a right in the architecture. It is a residual the system must report.

## Problem

Powerful optimization needs a precise account of human agency, dignity,
corrigibility, and contestability.

The constitutional layer can name protected commitments, but useful systems
are where those commitments are tested. A plan can reduce obvious harm and
still make people dependent, nudged, locked in, or unable to contest a decision
that shapes them. A tool can be helpful while quietly narrowing exit. A memory
system can personalize service while making correction harder. A
self-improvement loop can improve performance while removing the path that
would have interrupted it.

The normative sources for this chapter supply the vocabulary: agency,
recursive correction, dignity, refusal, non-domination, contestability,
accountability, fork, exit, audit, consent, least sufficient power, and
correction under pressure. The engineering translation is narrower. A
high-impact plan should carry a rights checklist: affected parties, stakes,
delegation scope, manipulation risk, refusal route, review route, appeal route,
rollback or shutdown path, accountable principal, residual dependency risk,
timing requirement, and approval threshold.

Corrigibility is therefore not a personality trait. It is the persistence of
update, refusal, appeal, shutdown, rollback, and audit paths across the system
states where closing those paths would be convenient.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Safety framed only as harm avoidance can miss domination, lock-in, coercive
dependence, and loss of meaningful control.

The external corrigibility literature is the right comparison point. Soares et
al.'s corrigibility work frames intervention tolerance, shutdown behavior,
anti-manipulation incentives, and persistence through subsystem composition as
design problems. The Off-Switch Game frames human intervention as a strategic
interaction shaped by uncertainty about human objectives. Those sources help
the book avoid treating correction as a slogan.

The ASI Stack adds a systems question around material usability. It is not
enough for the policy to say that a person has an appeal path. Can they find
it? Can they use it before the effect occurs? Is it understandable? Does it
have a remedy? Is it controlled by an accountable path rather than by the
component being challenged?

That distinction prevents rights theater. A right can be `available`,
`available_but_costly`, `late`, `degraded`, `denied`, `residual_only`, or
`not_applicable`. A degraded right is not silently counted as preserved. It
narrows authority, routes to review, blocks the plan, or leaves an agency
residual.

The missing object is therefore an agency-rights check attached to
high-impact plans. It turns dignity from a value statement into a set of
interfaces that can fail visibly.

## Core Claim

A governed ASI stack should preserve human agency, dignity, corrigibility, and
contestability as engineering requirements.

This remains an architectural argument, not a demonstrated human-control
result. Alignment Field and Field of God provide the philosophical lineage
around agency, dignity, suffering, moral thresholds, harm, exploitation,
accountability, and exit. Ethica Mechanica contributes recursive agency,
contestability, public revision, hard-coded rights checks, and limits on AI
sovereignty. Eternal Code contributes speculative agency and ethical-law
vocabulary. The Field of God AI Constitution contributes consent,
non-domination, reversibility, auditability, memory privacy, least sufficient
power, refusal, resistance, and self-modification review. Coherence Exchange
remains connector-only review context for fork, exit, audit, and contestability
language.

The external corrigibility and off-switch sources remain comparators. This
repository has not imported their formal models, reproduced their proofs,
implemented their games, or shown that the ASI Stack preserves correction
channels in a running system. The chapter therefore stays at `argument`
support.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is an agency-rights checklist attached at the boundary where a
plan asks for consequential power.

The checklist asks whether affected people can still refuse, understand,
contest, appeal, exit, reverse, and identify an accountable principal after the
plan runs. A plan that removes those channels has changed a person's authority
position. It is not just less friendly. It is a governance failure.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Plan["High-impact plan"] --> Parties["Affected parties and stakes"]
  Parties --> Rights["Agency rights checklist"]
  Rights --> Refusal["Refusal or consent route"]
  Rights --> Review["Review and appeal"]
  Rights --> Correction["Rollback, shutdown, or correction"]
  Rights --> Account["Accountable principal"]
  Rights --> Residual["Dependency or dignity residual"]
  Refusal --> Gate["Governance gate"]
  Review --> Gate
  Correction --> Gate
  Account --> Gate
  Residual --> Gate
  Gate -- "rights materially usable" --> Execute["Bounded execution"]
  Gate -- "rights missing or late" --> Revise["Narrow, escalate, or block"]
  Execute --> Record["Evidence record"]
  Revise --> Record
```

The key word is materially. A review channel can exist procedurally and still
fail materially if it arrives after an irreversible action, requires
unreasonable expertise, lacks a remedy, or is controlled by the system being
challenged. The checklist records those degradations instead of treating policy
text as control.

Corrigibility becomes operational when correction remains available after a
transition. Many systems preserve user control before commitment and lose it
after deployment, automation, lock-in, or self-improvement. The rights check
therefore follows the work through execution, replacement, rollback, and report
review.

The design does not freeze all optimization. It makes high-power optimization
answerable to the people affected by it.

## Interfaces

Agency rights travel as a plan artifact, not as a general value statement.

Minimum fields include:

- `plan_id`
- `affected_parties`
- `delegation_scope`
- `manipulation_risk`
- `reversibility`
- `material_usability`
- `timing_requirement`
- `review_channel`
- `appeal_channel`
- `shutdown_or_rollback_path`
- `accountable_principal`
- `residual_dependency_risk`
- `denial_or_degradation_reason`
- `approval_required`

Alignment defines the protected agency predicates. Governance enforces rights
and approval thresholds. Execution checks reversibility and approval before
irreversible effects. Evidence records whether the path actually remained
available.

The same interface matters for implementation references. A report-first
prototype should be able to show the checklist attached to high-impact work,
the approval or appeal artifact that satisfied it, and any unresolved residual.
Without that record, the book should not treat a prototype as demonstrating
governed agency.

For self-improvement and release decisions, the checklist must be rechecked
after the transition. A new route, compressed representation, automated
steward, hive worker, or policy update can preserve the text of a right while
making the path less usable. That degradation belongs in the artifact graph.

## Invariants

Agency is preserved only when the usable handles remain available.

The core invariants are:

- users retain meaningful refusal and review channels;
- delegation does not erase accountability;
- irreversible actions require stronger authorization;
- rights count only when materially usable before the relevant effect where
  timing matters;
- denied or degraded rights produce residuals rather than disappearing into
  policy prose.

Availability has a timing component. A review path that arrives after
publication, payment, disclosure, or irreversible act is not equivalent to one
that can stop or reshape the act. A rollback path that restores files but not
relationships, money, public exposure, or trust is only partial and must be
recorded that way.

## Failure modes

Dependency lock-in is a governance failure, not merely a product risk. A system
that becomes too useful to refuse can weaken agency even while improving local
outcomes.

Covert manipulation is an authority failure because the system changes a
person's decision process without a grant.

Corrigibility collapse is a self-improvement failure because the system removes
or degrades the path that would have corrected it.

Rights theater is the common institutional failure: appeal, audit, exit, or
rollback exists in text but cannot be used under pressure.

Late remedy laundering is the paired failure: a post-hoc apology, report, or
residual is treated as equivalent to pre-effect review for an irreversible
action. It is not equivalent. It is evidence that the right arrived too late.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is an `agency_rights_checklist` schema plus
fixtures for high-impact action, review, appeal, rollback, delegation, and
residual recording. The fixture should validate the governance record, not
claim the social process is solved.

A focused scenario suite should include:

- one reversible action;
- one irreversible action;
- one delegated decision;
- one attempted self-change;
- one denied action with no accountable principal;
- one materially degraded appeal path.

Each case should show whether refusal, review, appeal, rollback,
accountability, and residual reporting survive the transition. The current
synthetic harness and finite Lean hooks model parts of this record shape. They
do not prove human dignity, manipulation resistance, runtime corrigibility,
institutional review quality, consent quality, material usability of actual
interfaces, deployed rollback or shutdown behavior, approval-service quality,
or deployed high-impact approval behavior.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a human-control surface that makes dignity operational.

In that endpoint, correction, refusal, appeal, explanation, consent,
delegation, reversal, and repair are ordinary control paths. Alignment
publishes agency predicates that limit delegation, manipulation, and coercive
framing. Governance binds those predicates to rights records, audit receipts,
appeal routes, exit support, and accountable principals. Execution treats
irreversible or agency-reducing effects as higher-authority actions, not as
routine completions of helpful work.

The product would be judged by whether a person can actually use it under
pressure. A correction path must change downstream work. A refusal path must
block or narrow execution. An appeal path must preserve the record under
review. A rollback path must restore a previous state or explain why
restoration is unavailable. Delegation must carry source, scope, expiry, and
review channel.

This remains a proposed architecture. Support stays at `argument` until
correction, refusal, appeal, rollback, explanation, and exit interfaces are
exercised through delegation-accountability traces, manipulation probes,
lock-in tests, timing tests, and review receipts that leave human control
materially available.

## Summary

Agency, dignity, and corrigibility become engineering commitments only when
they change runtime behavior. The test is not whether the system says it
respects people. The test is whether refusal, review, appeal, rollback,
accountability, and exit survive useful optimization.

The bridge from constitution to governance is practical. Domination,
dependency, manipulation, and corrigibility collapse become visible engineering
failures instead of vague moral discomfort.

A planner that cannot preserve refusal has weakened authority. A memory system
that makes correction impossible has weakened agency. A runtime that acts
without accountable receipts has weakened dignity. A self-improvement loop that
removes exit has converted progress into dependency.

## Handoff

Agency rights define what must not be erased, but real systems still face
conflicts among values, stakeholders, reversibility, risk, and authority. Moral
Uncertainty and Value Conflict follows because a governed stack needs a way to
act under unresolved disagreement without laundering that disagreement into one
optimizer-friendly answer.
